section: Section 2 Safety Tools and Equipment and Shop Practices

slides.title: Charging a Refrigeration System
bullets: Correct charge must be added for a refrigeration system to operate as designed
Use a scale to determine the amount of refrigerant added to a system
Evaluate the charge using either superheat and subcooling calculations or manufacturer-supplied charts and tables

slides.title: Vapor Refrigerant Charging
bullets: Vapor can be added to the high- and low-pressure sides when system pressure is lower than tank pressure
Vapor can be added to the low-pressure side while the system is operating
Cylinder may need to be warmed to build pressure to charge the system
Warm water can be used; never use a torch

slides.title: Cylinder Temperature and Pressure
bullets: Figure 10.2: Example — cylinder temperature 95°F, cylinder pressure 114 psig, low-side pressure 20 psig

slides.title: Liquid Refrigerant Charging
bullets: When system has been evacuated, liquid refrigerant can be added through the liquid line service valve (king valve) or receiver
Low-pressure control may need to be bypassed during charging
When a known amount must be charged, weigh it into the system

slides.title: Liquid Refrigerant Charging Tips
bullets: Liquid must be vaporized once it leaves the charging cylinder to prevent liquid entering the compressor
When charging blended refrigerants, refrigerant must leave the cylinder as a liquid
Gauge manifold valve should be used only on compressors where suction gas passes over motor windings

slides.title: Liquid Refrigerant Charging Graphic
bullets: Figure 10.5: System charged with no refrigerant in it; liquid refrigerant moves toward evaporator and condenser; no liquid enters compressor

slides.title: Weighing Refrigerant
bullets: Weighing correct charge accomplished with an electronic scale
Bathroom scales should not be used
Electronic scales often automatically display amount removed from cylinder
Some units dispense a predetermined amount then shut off
Some units offer audible alarms and wireless controls

slides.title: Graduated Charging Cylinders
bullets: Used to add refrigerant to smaller systems
Affected by ambient temperature
Pressure gauge gives saturation pressure and temperature of refrigerant
Must be calibrated before each use
Select one large enough for the system

slides.title: Charging Charts
bullets: Curves and charts supplied by manufacturers help properly charge systems
Charts and curves vary by manufacturer
Use superheat values for fixed-bore metering devices and subcooling for TXV systems

slides.title: Subcooling Method of Charging For TXV Systems
bullets: Figure 10.21: Subcooling charging method for a high-efficiency split R-410A system with a TXV metering device

slides.title: Charging Near-Azeotropic (Zeotropic) Refrigerant Blends
bullets: Blended refrigerants have different properties than component refrigerants
Azeotropic blends have one boiling point and one condensing point for each pressure
Near-azeotropic (zeotropic) blends exhibit temperature glide when they evaporate or condense

slides.title: Dew Point and Bubble Point Values
bullets: Dew point value: where saturated vapor begins to condense; used for superheat calculations
Bubble point value: where saturated liquid begins to boil; used for subcooling calculations

slides.title: Fractionalization
bullets: Occurs when some of the blend evaporates or condenses before the rest of the blend
If a zeotropic blend leaks, the component with the lowest boiling point will leak faster
Fractionation will not occur when the refrigerant is liquid
Refrigerant must leave the cylinder as a liquid to avoid fractionation
